visualGoal: Honestly frame a low-density buyer question. Show the ONE verified product proof (the non-FFB Honeycomb Alpha baseline) and keep every FFB unit text-only until a real photo is verified. No procedural art, no unverified gear photos, no forced cards.

realProductPhotosNeeded.product: MOZA AY210 Force Feedback Yoke (base + MFY handle)
realProductPhotosNeeded.notes: A file exists at this path on disk but is NOT in src/data/verified-product-images.json, so it was REMOVED from the article body this pass (was previously embedded in a shotgrid + two gearpicks). The AY210 is the headline product but stays text-only until a verified studio photo is confirmed and added to the manifest. This is the single biggest visual blocker before certification.

DIY/community FFB stick base (~9 Nm). Text-only in the body. No gear page; Amazon search link only. Note: joystick base, not a yoke — kept in the 'options' landscape, not compared head-to-head with the Alpha yoke.

editorialCompositePrompts.prompt: Realistic desk flight-sim bench at soft window light, an open aluminum-profile mounting plate with 4 bolt holes waiting for a real yoke product overlay, curator Mac Donovan resting a hand on the mount and looking skeptical-but-fair, MSFS running on a single monitor in the background out of focus, no fake branded hardware, no fake UI text, 16:9 editorial cover conveying 'is it worth it?'.
editorialCompositePrompts.negativePrompt: fake logos, fake FFB yoke, warped controls, impossible ergonomics, tiny unreadable text, procedural gradient art, invented product renders

infographicPrompts.title: What force feedback actually adds
content: Trim forces — feel it, trim it to neutral
Air loads + gusts react to the air
infographicPrompts.style: IgnitionSim command-center chart, large readable text, flight-deck blues/amber, spring-vs-servo split, Alpha photo as the 'spring' row marker

infographicPrompts.title: FFB yoke vs spring yoke — who buys which
content: Buy FFB: fly for control feel, trim by feel, will mount rigidly, accept maturing software
Buy spring: still building fundamentals, airliner-first, no rigid mount, value more cockpit per dollar
infographicPrompts.style: Two-column decision card, ~$848 vs ~$280 price banners, honest-broker tone

curatorScenePrompts.prompt: Realistic adult flight-sim curator Mac Donovan in a tidy home cockpit corner, one hand on a rigid yoke mount, thoughtful honest-broker expression, product overlay space beside him, practical not fantasy, 4:5 portrait.

openQuestions: Can we verify an exact MOZA AY210 studio photo so the headline product can appear visually and the article can be certified?
